text: There’s a scene where the entire cast is celebrating Akshay and Arjun laughing, while Ranveer is on the verge of breaking down. author: @ashapillay1638
text:
Dragging Dhurandhar by samosa critics or Stan Twitter or reddit because it isn’t earning like Animal, Pathaan, Chava or War is just lazy thinking. 

Dhurandhar isn’t built for fanfare; it’s built for filmmaking. It’s a mood, a craft, a slow burn — not a 500-crore firecracker. 

And thank God for that, because if every film tried to be a “record breaker,” Bollywood would have zero space left for actual artistry.

Box office is a statistic.
Craft is cinema

Box office tells you how many people watched something.
Craft tells you why it deserved to be made.

Dhurandhar feels like an Inglourious Bastards or Schindler's list in an era obsessed with the Avengers—craft-focused cinema trying to survive in a race ruled by numbers.

text:
What many people don't realize here is that yrf and red chillies have a huge fan following (read audiences) amongst Pakistanis, Bangladeshis, middle-east (gulf) people all over the world including in USA and UK. They simply don't want to lose that business, which is why they hide truth and make-up absurd stories of friendships/love between Indian spies and ISI agents as in Tiger... , Pathan, etc. Even, many Indian Muslims won't like films like Dhurandhar, such is their inner connection with Pakistan. Revealing ugly, brutal and gory truth of Pakistan thus means you are going to lose a big chunk of business both in India and abroad, which these production houses are not willing to afford. So, truth is secondary, making money is their prime goal. 

Another point is - Dhurandhar is not the first film that exposes Pakistan. Sarfarosh in 1999 clearly pointed fingers to Pakistan for the cross border terrorism. But such well crafted movies that also presents the ugly truth of Pakistan in rational and logical way are indeed rare from Bollywood

author: @ehehehe-
text:
Pakistani here! Watched the first show of this masterpiece when it was released in Canada. I was shocked by the accuracy of the depiction of Lyari, Karachi as a city, and even Islamabad (my city). 

What’s wrong is wrong and I do not stand with wrong, even if it comes from my own country. I did not find it offensive at all. Instead, the way they portrayed the gangs, politicians, and Balochi kids being killed by ISI was quite accurate, and toned down imo (the reality is even worse). 

Overall, the best spy movie to come out of India. Putting all the political stuff aside, it’s genuinely a well-made movie. Even politically, it’s quite accurate, unfortunately (yeah Pak is f*ed up).

Bonus: Hawa Hawa was so nostalgic😭

author: @alldramaloverindian145
text:
The script was too good and actors were just a tadka for the royal  Shahi biriyani..
The movie draws inspiration from real documented incidents and a story woven out of it.All  of these stories are individually correct .They happened in different time zones.The director  has woven a fictional story connecting all these links.

1999- Hijack of the airplane happened.Mr.Ajit Doval was personally present in the negotiations.Masood Ahmad was released in exchange of all the passengers.Jaswant Singh,the then external affairs minister and an ex- - major  was also there for negotiations with the terrorist and he personally escorted the militants.The militants wanted someone with high authority to take decisions on the spot and not wait for clearance.At that time ,BJP was at the centre with AB Vajpayee as Prime Minister.Mostbof the important dialogues Madhavan said are actually said by Mr.Doval on record.

Fake currency -Politician P.Chidambaram and his son Karthi along with  the IAS officer Arvind Mayaram responsible for giving tender to a British company for the raw materials needed for currency printing.This British firm was blacklisted and still given an extension for this contract.A lot of top officials were involved but never got apprehended.

The Khanani brothers ran the grand money laundering scheme by Hawala.One was killed in suspicious circumstances  in Karachi in  2016 and the other was  extradited to the USA and in jail from where he was released in 2020.Different time zones.

2008 Mumbai attacks -The main person responsible for this was Illiyas Kashmiri.He rejected to work under  Masood Azhar.lliyas kashmiri was played by Arjun Rampal as Dr.Iqbal.The warning for the attacks were given by US officials three years before the attacks.As per news reports ,some say that India has been warned 26 times ,some claim that the warning was not quite strong .RAW also got information but couldn't connect the dots.We have opposing reports from news outlets here.Congress was the ruling party at that time.Illiyas however as of now was killed in 2011 drone strikes by USA.He is reported dead until proven otherwise.

Spies from every country  live in enemy countries for many years.They get married ,have a respectable job and kids but they always pass intel to their parent country.The spies are only known by their failures not success.We never get to know about the good spies.Going to  a hostile country and working against the laws of that country need iron balls.

Because of Morarji Desai ,I K gujral, both the  then ex- prime ministers as well as Hameed Ansari ,Ambassador of India to Iran, a lot of RAW agents were killed.They gave the enemy country a list with the whole details  of RAW agents who were targetted in Pakistan and Iran .For this Prime Minister Morarji Desai received Pakistan's highest civilian award, the Nishan-e-Pakistan.Again  different time zones.Mr.Desai thought with his own short sightedness that RAW was not needed.

Here also Ranveer Singh honey traps a girl ,is sincere to her but haven't told her anything.Similar to Major Mohit Sharma who lived amongst terrorists to gather information and then returned safely.Ranveer Singh is a prison inmate on a death row who is used by the RAW to infiltrate the gangs / militant groups.It is similar to how Chinese send their most dangerous criminals as first line of defence in borders to fight with the neighbouring country .They are not a loss to the nation if they get killed or tortured.Here also Ranveer Singh has only 1 contact in Lyari ,his Indian handler and a colleague who joined the rival Babu Dakait gang  as another infiltrator.RS doesnt know any other Indian agent apart from him .Even if he gets killed,the country's security won't be compromised.He is only a tool.It may sound cruel but that's how it works.Its far from glamorous.Throughout the movie ,Ranveer's trauma,fear ,panic ,PTSD ,loneliness is visible with no words.He was given quite  less dialogues than Akshay Khanna and other antagonists and still he showed his great acting skills with his eyes.Akshay still got subtle filmy dialogues.The life of a spy is definitely not glamorous as shown in other " spy" Bollywood movies.The agents ( assets) in reality  always have the risk of getting raped,tortured brutally in unimaginable ways.Death is liberating for them.

Some were apprehended like Ravinder Kaushik who was tortured and killed  and Kulbhushan Jadav who is still serving the prison.Different time frames.Or we have double agents like Rabinder Singh who was an army officer and a RAW  officer ,yet was a CIA mole.He was indicted but ran to the USA who gave him asylum but later withdrew  financial support and washed their hands off him . Soon he was killed mysteriously in a car crash in 2016( not shown in part 1 movie ).His wife was also killed.Daughter is a citizen in USA again emphasizing that crime never pays.It could either be CIA 

The recent shooting in Bondi Beach in Australia was done by a father and son living in Australia for 27 years .What do u have to say about it ?I'm sure Australian intelligence knew about it a long time back but failed to prevent it right ??

author: @EmaanBalouch
text:
I grew up in Lyari. Not the romanticised, gritty aesthetic Lyari people like to reference online, i mean the real one. The one where gunshots weren’t headlines but were background noise. Where gang wars weren’t legends, they were calendar events. Every few months or so the streets reset themselves with another round of chaos and another night of everyone pretending they weren’t listening to what was happening outside.

But here’s the secret no one on the outside ever really understands:
Lyari, ironically, felt safe.

Yes! Safe!

Because when you grow up surrounded by gangs who call themselves your “protectors,” you’re conditioned to believe it. The gangs sold themselves as our guard dogs, Robin Hoods in slippers and shalwar kameez convincing us that whatever they were doing, they were doing it for the town, for us, for “their people.” So while the rest of Karachi treated Lyari like a forbidden zone, rickshaw walas flinching the second you said “Lyari chalo ge?” we walked our streets like nothing could touch us.

I still remember my 6th grade annual party. I was dressed, excited, ready to go and then the Lyari Operation began. The one led by Chaudhry Aslam. The one that split open the entire underworld like a wound. Suddenly, my party didn’t exist; instead, there were bursts of gun fire, the echo of hand grenades, walls shaking from shelling. And the strangest part?

We didn’t even flinch.
We were kids and we didn’t flinch.

That’s how normalised the madness was.

It wasn’t until after that 8–9 day operation when the big names fled to Dubai and Iran, when the neighbourhood went eerily quiet that the people of Lyari realized what they had been living inside. It was like stepping out of a dark room, only to realize you’d been sitting in pitch black the entire time.

Did it end?
People like to pretend it did. It makes them feel hopeful.

But anyone who lives here knows the truth,
It didn’t end. It only shrank. It went quiet, not clean.

And as for films? I always wished someone would make one that truly shows what Lyari was the fear, the loyalty, the delusion, the danger, the strange sense of belonging. Someone did try, with the film about Chaudhry Aslam. But let’s be honest, no filmmaker from the same country will ever go all the way. Not when telling the whole truth means pointing fingers upward. Not when it means upsetting political parties who would rather rewrite history than be exposed by it.

And the narrative that gang wars ended because of some “Indian spy”?
That’s not just wrong 
it’s insulting to everyone who lived through the real reasons.

And ever since I heard about Dhurandar, there’s been this excitement. A part of me feels like, finally, our story is being treated with the attention and weight it deserves. I’m genuinely happy that the narrative is getting its due recognition, not as a footnote, not as a rumour, not as a political deflection but as an actual story worth telling.

I haven’t watched the movie yet.
Because well har cheez ham tak pohanchne mein time lagati hai.

But when it does reach me, you best believe I’ll be back with an update.

author: @Shivangi1524
text:
Honestly speaking, the movie is not portrayed in favour of Pakistan, obviously. However, the timeline of events is largely accurate, and the depiction of the characters closely resembles their real-life counterparts. The film also shows the attack in Pakistan that was carried out by ISI members in the very country that shelters them.
That said, the romantic angle between the spy and the minister’s daughter is unaccounted for and appears to have been added solely to add drama to the storyline.
Additionally, the movie does not show that the spy stopped the gang wars. He was merely present alongside SP Aslam, fighting side by side with him. This portrayal seems to exist only for cinematic purposes, since the spy is presented as the hero of the film. The complete truth is not revealed, even though we know that SP Aslam did an exceptional job in eliminating hooliganism in the area—on his own.

author: @KhizzarIslam
text:
​@Sceptilemaniac2
Studying NLP doesn't give you authority and it certainly doesn't let you put a label of "written by chatgpt" on a well-written narrative. 

However, if you choose to make such an accusation, the burden of proof is on YOU to prove that its AI. Speculation is not evidence and neither is studying NLP. 

Authorship attribution to youtube comments and short informal texts is an open problem in your own field btw. Disagree with content if you want, but calling it AI without proof isnt analysis. Its merely a lazy dismissal.

author: @Sceptilemaniac2
text:
​@KhizzarIslamalright you're right, the proof of burden does fall on me. This information does seem very human but the structure is definitely AI. If it wasn't written by LLMs, I can be sure it was heavily edited by one. 
Proving that is an interesting thought to me and I might actually try and do it, just as a experiment. I'd actually welcome the possibility that this is not Ai-written but some stylistic choices like beginning paragraphs with questions, the "negative, negative, positive" thing, anaphora and rhetorical questions as they are used here feel very AI like. They dramatize the story for sure but it makes it feel like the teller is using AI to at least polish the text if not completely generate it.

author: @Sceptilemaniac2
text:
​@EmaanBalouchSorry for the doubt, maybe I am wrong but some stylistic choices do feel very AI-like to me. Especially in the first half of the text. 
This defence however feels completely human and I mean.. you can see the difference in the style of writing. This sounds very organic, but the original comment sounds too structured, almost sterilized.

author: @KhizzarIslam
text: ​​@Sceptilemaniac2stylistic choices like "negative, negative, positive", anaphora, rhetorical questions have existed for hundreds of years. Long before AI was even talked about as a possibility. You'll see better writing styles in Doyle's and Dickens'  or in works of any known writer for that matter  than a normal person would make with AI these days. Furthermore, there is no trademark on structure or writing styles. Anybody can use it and people have been using it. And your statement about how its dramatic but it proves that its AI to make it look polished doesn't hold water. Because then according to you, any script, book,  novel, short story, poetry, prose, or even a a dramatic sentence ever produced was written by AI. If you're taking proving its AI as a challenge then be my guest but that also is an admittance of the fact that you haven't proved it yet. Hence, I think you should refrain from trying to insist its AI (content or structure). Peace.

author: @preciouslyp
text:
Some points from my side: 
1) before starting watching I didn't even pay attention to the run time. At one point i thought, "so much has been shown then is it nearing the end or what" just to find out that only 47 minutes have passed and the movie is 3 hours +. Time didn't bother me more than the fact that wow so much has been shown already so what's gonna happen. 

2) the age gap bothered me BEFORE watching the film. But as you rightly said they addressed it in the movie and in glad they did. Plus, her character wouldn't fall for him that easily if she weren't a teenager. So the characters know about the age gap. She had the choice (without any threat) to walk away and she chose to stay. 

3. If they fit all the dialogues as per that era, it'd have seemed more like a documentary than an action drama film. At least to me. So I believe some level of dramatisation was needed and incorporated well. 

4. What we millennials or older generations think is easily understood in the film might not be the case for all other kinds of viewers—younger or non-hindi speakers. So in that regard, that easy feeding of information and keeping the narrative linear and stable helped. 

5. Everybody acted so so damn well. Even the smaller roles like that of gaurav gera. But but but. 
Chef's kiss for Akshaye Khanna. He ate and left no crumbs. He looked so hat goddamn. Black IS his colour. What a manly aura. 😭😘

author: @syedmarwanmahmud
text:
It honestly baffles me how people are even attempting to compare Ranveer Singh’s Dhurandhar act to Ranbir😂 Kapoor’s performance in Animal. The comparison is baseless

Ranbir Kapoor as Ranvijay Singh Balbir delivered a performance loaded with diversity, depth, intensity, and range. In Animal, he didn’t just play a character,  he transformed into six distinct psychological versions of the same man, each with its own body language, emotional arc, command, and layered expression.
He was the spine of the film  carrying it with the presence and dominance of a lion. And the results speak clearly: nearly 80% of Animal’s massive box office success came from Ranbir’s sheer screen power and performance.

Meanwhile, Ranveer in Dhurandhar wasn’t even the primary lead and the film couldn’t match even the opening numbers of Animal, pulling in roughly ₹28 crore against Animal’s ₹60 crore day-one thunder.

So, let’s stop forcing this comparison. Ranbir’s performance in Animal belongs to a league of its own one that isn’t reached by hype, loud marketing, or blind fan debates.

author: @syedmarwanmahmud
text:
​@snek4699 “Animal was terrible."😂
Yeah, yeah  we’ve heard that line from every self-certified film critic who suddenly thinks opinions are facts😂

If Animal was so bad, why is Dhurandhar being compared to it instead of the other way around?😂😂
Why isn’t anyone saying “Ranbir’s performance reminds me of Ranveer in Dhurandhar”?

Because whether you like it or not, Ranbir delivered a performance that shook audiences, triggered debates, broke records, and set a new acting benchmark.
Meanwhile Dhurandhar’s legacy discussion lasted about as long as its opening weekend numbers. 
Even after two years of release Animal is still discussed because of the impact it has created.

author: @syedmarwanmahmud
text:
​@saurabhkanojia5680 If hype alone made blockbusters, every big studio film would explode  but we’ve seen the opposite happen repeatedly. Jayeshbhai Jordaar, Circus,  Shamshera too, Samrat Prithviraj  massive promotions, zero traction.
So clearly, audiences don’t show up just because someone advertised well  they show up when something hits them.

And Animal didn’t “perform”,  it detonated.
Why?
Because it wasn’t a safe, polished, predictable Bollywood formula.
It was chaotic, loud, flawed, aggressive, disturbing, emotional  and most importantly: unapologetically original.
People weren’t watching it for a storyline they’ve seen 500 times 
They were watching it because it felt different, risky, uncomfortable, and unpredictable.
And whether someone loved it or hated it  they FELT something.
That’s the currency that films almost never achieve.
& Regarding 'Performance ' & Ranbir,
You don’t pull multiple repeat audiences with “marketing.”
You don’t create obsession, arguments, breakdown scenes, viral edits, massive discourse and universal acting praise because of a poster.

That happens because:
 The acting was on another level,
 the character was layered, unpredictable, and psychologically complex.Ranbir didn’t act he consumed the role along with bobby deol 
That's why Animal became the conversation.
Not the marketing,Not luck
The impact that it created, still its in the conversation even after  years of its release 

So let’s not rewrite history to comfort personal preference.
Some films succeed because they’re safe Some succeed because they’re smart And some succeed because the lead actor delivers a performance powerful enough to shake the entire audience and Animal falls into that category.

You don’t have to like it  but denying why it worked won’t change what it did.

text:
Dhurandhar is a great film and an amazing experience but here are a few issues I had with it:

First off, the film is more alternate history or historical fiction than "based on real events". The Lyari gangs were actual gangs in Karachi but their crackdown was a Pakistan-internal reality, handled largely by Pakistani authorities, with little to no known R&AW or Indian intel involvement. The story basically takes those events and inserts an Indian element in it, and spins the story around in a "26/11 revenge" plot which we have already seen in Phantom and other films but something which never actually happened in real life. R&AW and other Indian intelligence never actually took revenge for 26/11, so now these plots feel more like cope and regret rather than cool and awesome. Instead, R&AW's actual operations are far more badass than these fictional ones, and making a movie based on those, especially with Aditya Dhar's direction, would’ve been much better.

More so while the film does connect the story with 26/11, the weapons used by lashkar in that attack were not sourced from the Baloch Liberation Army. They were sourced separately by Lashkar's own militant network with heavy ISI involvment. So this portrayal in the film that the weapons used for 26/11 were baloch weapons is fiction. And then the film still tries to sympathize with the Baloch struggle, (especially with that abrupt water-poisoning/burial scene). 

Basically, the movie is pretty entertaining, but it’s also aimed more at viewers who aren’t deeply familiar with the Lyari operation or the real 26/11 timeline. Anyone who is familiar will be able to see that this isn’t as as "based on real events" as the makers claim. 

Also, a small plot nitpick: if Hamza (Ranveer Singh’s character) already knew Rehman Dakait was linked with ISI and indirectly enabling threats against India, he could’ve just let him die when he had the chance. Instead he goes through a whole rescue arc only to kill him later after the attack happens. Not a huge issue but definitely a head scratcher.


Lastly I would say that Akshaye Khanna outshines everyone else in this film. Most of the film is carried by his acting performance and his character's sheer aura. Overall fun watch, solid performances, just don’t take the “based on real events” tag too seriously.

author: @ChaosCoffeee
text:
Being interested in the dark world of terrorism, drug and politics, Dhurandhar is an absolute masterpiece. Ahmed Omar Sheikh was among the three terrorist that India negotiated during IC-814, Raj Kumar Rao played his character in the movie called Omerta, he was a London School of Economics graduate. Seems pretty impossible in real life but Dhurandhar tried to connect all the dots together. I don't know about others, I absolutely loved the movie. 

Talking about the dots, ISI pushing fake currency through Nepal and Punjab is a public knowledge but the move to ban old 500 and 2000 denominations and at the same time pushing digital money into mainstream (upi) and then again withdrawing 2000 rupees notes from circulation can't be just a political move. I was so furious while standing in the line to withdraw 4000/day. Thanks to Doval he gave us the "Unknown Gunmen" phenomenon from Pakistan to Canada, even the Guradian reported on it 🫰🏼. Waiting for part 2.

P.S: I don't think general public is yet ready for non-linear timeline, it was underwhelming that way.

text: First half is intense. Sara Arjun is a miscast and the romance sequence are the only drag till now, still nowhere near as bad. While premature to say this, Dhar's scope of storytelling here would have better suited a 6-7 part miniseries. That is a compliment here, that such a long film still manages to be so densely filled with information that it feels like it would have been even better as a series.

author: @prityrag
text:
I watched this movie yesterday, solo, as a birthday gift to myself and boy was that a fantastic decision or what. I. Thoroughly enjoyed every bit of this movie. I was hitting, laughing cheering and crying. I didn't want it to end and I might be the first to buy the ticket for its next chapter. The story is so complex that I kept changing sides depending on who Ranveer is fighting with. 
I'm not a fan of Ranveer Deepika brigade, but he was outstanding in this role. It was so good that he didn't do his over the top acting that is almost irritating. He was so restrained in fact I thought he had just sinked in to the character. Loved the scene where they go to get arms from the Baloch army and dance and laugh when they play with guns. Crazy scene and it's crazy how I could just enjoy scenes that are actually pretty dark in reality. 

Akshay Khanna owned the movie. It was him and everyone after that. 

And the lines that are super/hyper patriotic as per some people, our sceen clapped at the last like as we got up. And I loved that. When real life agents dedicate their lives for country, we shouldn't be ashamed of being that at all❤

author: @ranyasonaedits8937
text:
As someone who genuinely loves espionage films—especially ones inspired by real events—I really value authenticity in storytelling. So going into this movie, I was excited. But I had no idea it was going to be a two-part release, and because of that, I walked in expecting a complete arc and a comeback moment for Ranveer Singh’s character. When that didn’t happen, I initially felt a little let down—but only because I didn’t know there was more coming.

With time to process, I do appreciate the intentional slow burn. The pacing almost forces you to feel the same patience and frustration the character experiences, which I think was done thoughtfully. Still, for me, some of the long action sequences felt tiring—not because they weren’t well done (they absolutely were)—but simply because I’m not someone who enjoys extended action for the sake of intensity. After a point, I found myself wanting the film to move forward into the emotional and strategic parts of the story rather than staying in repeated combat beats.

But I love that the filmmakers attempted something new—an ambitious, grounded espionage narrative that isn’t structured like the usual Bollywood formula. This doesn’t feel like Part 1 of a two-film format—it feels like one big story divided in the middle. And even though I wish the first part had one more twist or emotional payoff, I respect the experiment. Connecting the story to real incidents we’ve lived through or read about gave the film weight, relevance, and a sense of truth.

However, there were moments where it felt like the narrative shifted more toward the politics and gang wars across the border rather than the Indian perspective I was hoping to stay anchored to. It wasn’t bad—but it did feel heavy and slightly repetitive at points.

Still, I did enjoy the film. And because this kind of filmmaking is rare in Indian cinema—especially in this tone and scale—I’m willing to give it space to evolve. Maybe my expectations were shaped by teasers and the typical structure of such films. Now that I know Part Two is coming, I’m genuinely curious to see how everything unfolds.

And one last thing: the most powerful moment in the film for me was the red screen with the real intercepted recordings from the 26/11 handlers. That scene didn’t need visuals, music, or dramatization—the reality itself was more than enough. I felt numb, heavy, almost sick to my stomach. It was the most emotional moment in the movie, and hearing those actual voices made everything suddenly shift from cinema to memory, from entertainment to collective trauma. That alone stayed with me long after the credits.

author: @Zaiaurangzaib
text:
Just watched Dhurandhar. Since the story is based on Lyari, a neighborhood in Karachi just a few kilometers from where I live, I was curious to see what version Bollywood would roll out about something we Karachiites actually witnessed during the 2008–09 era.

1. The repeated idea that whoever rules Lyari rules Karachi is absolutely wrong in the movie. They have no idea about the real political power game in Karachi during 2008–09. The Lyari gang war existed, but it was only one small part of a much bigger landscape. Lyari itself is only 0.17 percent of the entire city.

2. No one in Pakistan celebrated the 26/11 Mumbai attacks. That is an impression Bollywood keeps feeding its audience. The whole “We will destroy India” gimmick does not work in Pakistan. People do not buy it. Even political and religious parties tried selling it and failed. People here are simply not interested in that chooran.

3. Lyari gang war was an internal conflict between local gangs fighting for territory and resources, tied to local and provincial politics. Linking it to 26/11 is just ridiculous. There is not even surface-level evidence for such a claim. But since that kind of narrative works for an Indian audience, Bollywood packaged and sold it.

4. In the story, India supposedly had all the intel about 26/11 and it still happened. It actually portrays how weak and incompetent the security layers were. The way the attackers keep communicating so easily on phone devices is not possible without inside help. In their self-victimization kink, they forgot what they themselves showed on screen.

5. The Indian spy repeatedly saying “Jisne Lyari pe hukumat ki, usne puray Karachi pe hukumat ki” is the funniest part. Do some actual research and you will understand who ruled Karachi in that timeline and what kind of power he had. Next time, come to us. We will tell you better stories this city holds.

Summary:
This is just another anti-Pakistan movie strategically designed to emotionally trigger the Indian audience through 26/11 and remind them that the-then government (Congress) did nothing, while the current government (BJP) is portrayed as the savior. Two dialogues make this intent obvious. One where the Pakistani General sarcastically says, “We were expecting some reaction from India but they did nothing.” The second when the Indian minister says, “Kabhi koi sarkar to aayegi jisko desh ki chinta hogi.”

author: @Shrad-n8g
text:
In Dhurandhar, Gaurav Gera’s “darling, darling, dil kyun toda” lands like a fractured whisper, half question, half ache, suspended in air long after it’s spoken.

It isn’t just dialogue; it becomes a mood. A soft rupture in the narrative where irony and emotion blur, and something absurdly simple suddenly feels devastatingly human.

Light in tone, heavy in echo, the turns a playful line into a lingering wound

author: @AtharvaParakh
text:
Spoilers Ahead 🚨🚨🚨🚨🚨


I believe for the second part we will see the young Ranveer Singh character who lives in Punjab and due to some reason gets heavily influenced by the separatist movement clashing with the events of Operation Blue Star which created a period of insurgency which then affects his psychology making him go on a death spree and finally getting caught by the authorities. The authorities in some way see a raw potential in him and train his character for years to then turn him into an asset which they can use against the enemy to stay one step ahead and get all information in advance.




Again this is just a theory. 

We can also see that how Ranveer Singh's character feels when Arjun Rampal's character plans for another attack which was very similar to the one shown in the movie and this time they choose the location to be Punjab.


Because all the events in the film are historically accurate I am excited to see how the makers will try to show Ranveer Singh's past and connect it to a historical event.

text:
This is such a divisive moment.

On one hand, I want to see the film in theaters and therefore let my money provide feedback to the industry that this is more like the kind of movies i am hoping to see instead of the fluff the industry has been releasing in theaters for 2 years.

On the other hand with a 3.5 hour run time and a second part coming in 6 months, is it worth waiting for an ott release where i can take multiple breaks? Not to mention with the recent sunny sanskari ki tulsi kumari and jolly llb 3, last 2 bigger movies to release, coming on ott in 2 months, chances are this will be on netflix by February.

author: @katana.kitsune
text:
I'm sorry Jammy, but I must point out that I found the ending far-fetched. The movie was fantastic, but I felt that the ending could've been a blast. I watched the movie only once in the theatre, so pardon me if I misinterpreted any of the scenes. But these are the specific points I found difficult to believe:

1. How is it possible that Rehman and his minions in Hamza's car were so soundly asleep at the same time, that Hamza was able to so smoothly take a detour/different route?
2. You're telling me they had guns under their seat the whole time and they only tried to strangle Hamza, leading to an accident, instead of even once trying to take out one firearm from under the seat and shooting him?
3. Why were the police not waiting for them in position? Why did the police have to arrive at the ambush site from elsewhere? Why were they not ready already? Better still, why go 5 kms down the road and have the entire strangling incident in the car? Why couldn't the police be waiting 2 kms down the road, so that Rehman and mininons would have less time to retaliate on Hamza?
4. Donga keeps walking towards the policemen even after the fat in his body is cradling multiple bullets. And this is believable?
5. The entire chase scene in the forest where SP Chaudhary Aslam kept shooting at Rehman and kept missing until the very end, was ridiculous.
6. What was that Agneepath-esque death scene where Hamza hangs Shaukat? Did that leafy rope thingy drop from heaven or it was just randomly lying there for Hamza to pick up and utilise?
7. What was the leafy lattice like thing in the background that SP Chaudhary kept throwing Rehman against? Where are all these props dropping from in the middle of a forest?

You're telling me that the final showdown of such a brilliant movie was this bullshit!? It's not lost on me that these tropes are used in every other movie. But Dhurandhar isn't every other movie, and I definitely don't want it to be. I loved the blood and gore, the testosterone, and the tashan. I am willing to suspend disbelief, but to what extent? This face-off with the police at the end let me down big time. I loved Animal, and I loved Dhurandhar. And so did millions of people around the globe, but if a movie has demerits then we call it out. Especially if the movie promises to give us a second part. If you don't want to be served mediocre food at a restaurant the second time, the food must be criticized before you leave the restaurant the first time.

Dhurandhar was a solid film. I can overlook editing, and I don't care about age gap, homophobia, propaganda— and whatever else today's woke mob is complaining about. It was a film made out of love, it deserves to be celebrated with love, and I say this out of love— The entire movie was a firework, but it fizzled out in the end, when it could've ended in an explosion. The highlight of the movie could've been better explored with just a few simple changes to the ambush plot and the subsequent chase scene. That's how I feel and I can only be honest about it. And I'm still wondering why honest reviewers like you and Shan are not calling it out. Or maybe it's me— I'm being too critical, when I possibly should've been more starstruck. I tried but failed, and that's THAT.

author: @obaidullahkhan8133
text: Dhurandhar is structured almost like a prelude—its entire narrative architecture seems designed to prepare the audience for a sequel where the Modi and Yogi analogues emerge as larger-than-life saviours. The first film repeatedly highlights failures, threats, and internal rot that other leaders supposedly ignored, creating a dramatic vacuum that only these two can eventually fill. Ajit Doval’s character reinforces this setup by stressing that “internal enemies” are the country’s real danger, using cues that heavily code this group as Muslims—whether through references to communal riots if “their leader” is touched, or by tying fake currency networks to slaughterhouses. His line about saving the incriminating file for a future leader who truly wants to “clean up” signals that Yogi will be that destined purifier, while remarks about the nation’s inaction after 26/11 hint that Modi will be the one to finally respond with unprecedented strength. Altogether, the movie lays a foundation that frames their eventual rise as not just political, but mythic—positioning the sequel to present them as heroic figures who alone can save the nation. This framing, intentionally aligns closely with BJP-style political storytelling and functions as soft propaganda for their larger-than-life image.

author: @MuhammadTalhaFarooqui
text:
Just watched Dhurandhar. Since the story is based on Lyari, a neighborhood in Karachi just a few kilometers from where I live, I was curious to see what version Bollywood would roll out about something we Karachiites actually witnessed during the 2008–09 era.

1. The repeated idea that whoever rules Lyari rules Karachi is absolutely wrong in the movie. They have no idea about the real political power game in Karachi during 2008–09. The Lyari gang war existed, but it was only one small part of a much bigger landscape. Lyari itself is only 0.17 percent of the entire city.

2. No one in Pakistan celebrated the 26/11 Mumbai attacks. That is an impression Bollywood keeps feeding its audience. The whole “We will destroy India” gimmick does not work in Pakistan. People do not buy it. Even political and religious parties tried selling it and failed. People here are simply not interested in that chooran.

3. Lyari gang war was an internal conflict between local gangs fighting for territory and resources, tied to local and provincial politics. Linking it to 26/11 is just ridiculous. There is not even surface-level evidence for such a claim. But since that kind of narrative works for an Indian audience, Bollywood packaged and sold it.

4. In the story, India supposedly had all the intel about 26/11 and it still happened. It actually portrays how weak and incompetent the security layers were. The way the attackers keep communicating so easily on phone devices is not possible without inside help. In their self-victimization kink, they forgot what they themselves showed on screen.

5. The Indian spy repeatedly saying “Jisne Lyari pe hukumat ki, usne puray Karachi pe hukumat ki” is the funniest part. Do some actual research and you will understand who ruled Karachi in that timeline and what kind of power he had. Next time, come to us. We will tell you better stories this city holds.

Summary:
This is just another anti-Pakistan movie strategically designed to emotionally trigger the Indian audience through 26/11 and remind them that the-then government (Congress) did nothing, while the current government (BJP) is portrayed as the savior. Two dialogues make this intent obvious. One where the Pakistani General sarcastically says, “We were expecting some reaction from India but they did nothing.” The second when the Indian minister says, “Kabhi koi sarkar to aayegi jisko desh ki chinta hogi.”

Here’s what I thought about the movie-
 Pros:
I loved the storytelling, acting, and technical aspects of the movie - the set design, cinematography, music, and background score. It’s an espionage thriller unlike anything made before.

Cons:
I went in expecting it to be a patriotic film like Uri, which it is not. It’s more of a political gangster movie set in Pakistan. A few scenes depicting terrorist attacks in India, and Ranveer’s character’s reaction to them, didn’t evoke any emotion in me. Also, the plot is quite predictable, with not much excitement about what will happen next, unlike Raazi.

author: @karthigaidipam
text:
Watched both finally in the theater. 

1. Casting: Barring Uzair Baloch, the rest were literally replicas of the people they represented! Be it, Rehman Dakait or SP Chaudhry Aslam or Ajay Sanyal (Ajith Doval) or even Jamal Jamali, all were chosen with care. The role of Uzair Baloch was reduced to a dummy, sorry, in both parts. Barring Sanyal and Jamali, the rest gets a brutal scene to alert the audience of their nature. 
2. The production set was a HIGHLIGHT for sure. Yes, the context was information-dense and there was a shit ton of violence into it but I found it justifying after hearing a few Pakistani content creators. Not sure if such dances (item numbers) are played out in a conservative Pakistan, the combination of Sara Arjun and Ranveer Singh makes sense in an Islamic society: I loved how it panned out between the two in Dhurandhar 
3. While Major Iqbal was phenomenal, Akshaye Khanna was the magician that mesmerized the public. Ranveer Singh couldn't replicate the entry song magic in Dhurandhar 2. The choreography was good in Dhurandhar 2 btw. 
4. The soundtracks were good. 
5. Donga, what a man! Hamza Ali Mazari played by Ranveer Singh had to keep it low-key. Thank God. 
6. The final scene was Shakespearean! 
7. Historical fiction, it is ... only thing the audience must keep in mind is this: Dakait had nothing to do with 26/11 in real life. 

I am sure people would want more of such movies on screen. As you say, a series can be started in the name of Dhurandhar and these details can be shared on an OTT platform. People were engaged with Dhurandhar until part 2 showed up and the latter is still ongoing.

Ranveer Singh played it as he should. I was very uncomfortable seeing him with Sara Arjun as she is not that far removed from the little girl with pigtails she was a few years ago. Yes she did fine but hard to overlook that she looks so young. The action was fantasies and the brutality was definitely warranted. The 26/11 part was especially powerful.

author: @Ami9299-maa
text:
The movie might be entertaining for a certain audience, but it is definitely not a well-made or authentic film. The characters are supposed to be Balochi, yet not a single word of Balochi is spoken. Imagine making Son of Sardaar without anyone speaking Punjabi—you would immediately question its authenticity.

The same applies to the music choices. Playing a 2024 Arabic rap song at a wedding in Balochistan is simply unrealistic and breaks immersion.

So yes, it may work as surface-level entertainment for some, but as a culturally authentic and well-researched film, it clearly falls short.

author: @StayvilleTown
text: I love watching your videos and I subscribed to your channel, like, years ago. This video though, has honestly disappointed me. There's absolutely no way you, as a say, a proper movie critic, watched this movie in a vacuum and have spoken not a single word on the ultra-nationalism, jingoism, and hyper masculinity the movie reflects. Especially in the current political climate. You mention politics in passing, but throughout the video, there's no reference to the stereotypical minority representation - which is something you've often talked about in your other reviews. Makes me honestly wonder how and why this movie analysis came out to be largely positive? Recent years have seen a plethora of hateful movies being put out, and this one, with this ensemble cast, further fuels it. But it's weird how your analysis is all praises about it, not commenting enough on how it affects the billion Indian population. I wonder if you purposely chose to only look at the technical aspects (I haven't watched Animal review), but if you did, it seems childish to look away and watch something as influential as this, in a complete vacuum. I'm utterly disappointed.

text:
The movie is well made, yes undoubtedly, but the propaganda aspect is so irritatingly infused in the story.
The character Ajay Danyal played by R Madhavan and the scenes involving him are for propaganda purposes mostly, it's such "in your face" propaganda that it's irritating.

The run time is a joke. The movie doesn't have enough content for the runtime. 
Some scenes between Ranveer and Akshay Khanna make no sense. One scene he's confronting Akshay to not help ISI terrorists as ISI kills Baloch people. A couple scenes later they are having a blast shooting guns. Like wut?
Everyone in this movie is such a poor shot, terrorists or gangsters or police. They have worse aim than stormtrooper, and that's saying something.
Fight scenes are way too overstretched and un-focused.
Characters seem like they wanna do kabbadi rather than kill.
The Akshay Khanna vs Sanjay Dutt fight is downright kindergarten shit, biting, sand throwing, tree hugging 😂.

This movie doesn't deserve the hype.
It's a well produced propaganda movie.

author: @WordsWitch
text: I just watched it today, and was so surprised at how good it was and how much I enjoyed it. The backstory reminded me a bit of The Grey Man. Akshay slayed so much that I might watch it again just to watch him again-what swag! It says something about his acting and charisma that he could overshadow both Ranveer Singh and Arjun Rampal. Some of the violence was just too much for me. Saw a lot of criticism about the casting of Sara Arjun due to the age gap, and I agree but after watching the film, I think she’s a natural actor and their chemistry was really sizzling. I can’t understand people saying this is “anti-Pakistani” and a propaganda movie when it literally shows two of the major terrorist attacks orchestrated by Pakistan on Indian soil. So what’s the propaganda here? Even the quote “a thousand cuts on India” is a real life quote by a Pakistani leader! So how is it anti-Pakistan to make a movie on their own terrorist activities? The movie doesn’t villainise the ordinary Pakistani citizens but clearly says it’s the ISI and the Pakistani mafia. But leaving all that aside, even in the cinematic and technical aspects, it was so well done. Didn’t feel bored even with the 3+ hour runtime and didn’t see any phone screens lit up in the theatre either, which is saying something about how engrossed people were in the movie.

author: @Rose-gq5uv
text: I went and watched this movie in theaters and I agree with this video, mostly. There were a few issues that I noticed which made this movie feel like it wasn’t well-handled by the director. 1. I did not appreciate the blurring of real and fictional elements in this movie. 26/11 was an incredibly difficult time and commentary made around it should be carefully handled which the movie did not. Especially with a movie so commercialized that it is accessible to everyone including those unable to critically think and separate real from fiction or spot out subtle propaganda. 2. I thought that the movie had an opportunity to genuinely invest in depicting the detriment of the gang wars in lyari. The workings of the gangs hurt india as well as the actual residents in lyari. The aspect of taking care of the community was not really a prevalent thing whatsoever. 3. Like the video says, i heavily dislike the favoring of a specific party in power. It feels very wrong to make blanket statements supporting one party especially when there is no significant analysis of pros or cons on both sides. It’s biased and again propaganda to people who are unable to clearly question the actions of governmental agencies.

author: @hkpasubscribe
text:
Using real footage is a cinematic trope . See the Oscar winning movie Saving Private Ryan . Used real footage with movie footage . I thought the real footage made this movie brilliant.. it made the trauma of the real event come alive for the audience .

If a story calling out an enemy country obsessed with killing our people and a government retaliating is propaganda to you .. you should seriously consider getting a different passport if you’re an Indian . This country is  doesn’t need people who won’t think of protecting their own .

author: @nothamdahmad
text: Great cinematic experience overall, but the historical accuracy was really sloppy in places. Showing a 2014 Mercedes in scenes set between 2004–2007 in Lyari completely breaks immersion. Either commit to the era properly or don’t pretend to be historically grounded. Also, mixing real figures like Shaheed Benazir Bhutto with fake party and politician names (PPP → “Pakistan Awami Party”, Zardari → “Aqib Zarwari”) only creates confusion. Either show real facts or go full fiction—this halfway approach misleads the audience.

author: @srinivasgullapalli135
text:
Too much Eulogizing review, while the whole First Part of Dhurandar is all about Akshaey Khanna elevating the entire film.

Is Indian IB & RAW are so dumb or what to infiltrate a Criminal into the Lyari' Gangs and their politicians n politics to get to espionage on ISI? - that too when he infiltrates into Lyari' Gang of Baloch's, it just about two tribal Baloch vs Pathan gangs, counterfeit guns and drug trafficking (and nothing to do with ISI and currency mafia) - this is a biggest and stupidest fault-line in the movie. And romantic angle a total waste and lengthy distraction and should have handled in a different way to have Ranveer-Rakesh Bedi alliance.

I don't know why it has been banned in Pakistan and Gulf countries ?!? - the first part is all about Pakistan Cherishing - when our so called IB infiltrate stands with a dumb & shocked face in the midst of ISI agent, Currency mafia and Gangster mafia rejoicing Mumbai Terror Attack on live TV(I am not surprised why Dhurandhar become a big hit in Pakistan inspite of ban - as it had almost all Cherishing moments for Pakistani's).

Finally, Film is well made in spite of its dumbest plot.

Akshaey Khanna is the real star of this first part, while Ranveer too did powerful acting as per characterization of a sleeping n helpless spy.

author: @islamkhowaja2565
text:
This is absolutely NOT okay! Using real Pakistani names like Zia-ul-Haq, Chaudhry Aslam, and Rahman Dakait in Indian movies just for controversy and money is completely disrespectful. Chaudhry Aslam was a REAL hero who fought criminals and terrorists, he's not some villain character for your entertainment!

Showing real places like Lyari Town to make it look "authentic" but then completely distorting the truth? Using "Bismillah" and religious words in cheap, disrespectful dialogues? That disgusting dialogue about Chaudhry Aslam you mentioned is absolutely shameful and false! This is propaganda, not cinema. You're using real names and places to spread lies and hatred just to fill your pockets.

This is pure brainwashing! If you want to make a crime movie, CREATE FICTIONAL CHARACTERS! Don't disrespect actual people who gave their lives fighting for their country. This isn't creative freedom, this is calculated propaganda to insult Pakistan and mislead your own audience. Real people and their sacrifices deserve respect, not to be turned into caricatures for your box office collection. Shame on the filmmakers for crossing every line of decency and respect!

author: @Sanatanwarrior-s3x
text:
धुरंधर फ़िल्म का कल ट्रेलर आया.... फ़िल्म में बहुत ज्यादा खून खराबा है... जिस सब्जेक्ट पर फ़िल्म है... उसमे खून खराबा नहीं होगा तो क्या होगा?

सब्जेक्ट क्या है?

पाकिस्तान की ISI, फ़ौज और आतंकवादी तंजीमो से लड़ने और उन्हें ख़त्म करने वाले अज्ञात हमलावरों... RAW के कुछ अनसुने, अनजाने Operations पर बनी है यह फ़िल्म..... कई घटनाये और पात्र सच्चे हैं.... हाँ उनके नाम बदले गए हैं... लेकिन उनका Getup इस तरह का है... कि आप आसानी से पहचान जाते हैं. माधवन, अक्षय खन्ना, संजय दत्त के Characters real हैं.... वहीं रणवीर का करैक्टर कई लोगों का मिला जुला है... कई agents की कहानियो को जिया है रणवीर ने.

जैसे ही ट्रेलर आया.... पाकिस्तान और उसके समर्थन करने वाले.... कुछ Youtuber आदि ने इसकी आलोचना करनी शुरू कर दी... कि इसमें तो इतनी हिंसा दिखाई गई है... यह तो समाज के लिए अच्छा नहीं है.

हिंसा दिखाई है... सही बात है..... लेकिन यह भी बहुत कम है.... जो सच में घटनाएं हुई हैं... वह इनसे कहीं ज्यादा हिंसक हैं.

एक उदाहरण लीजिये.... अर्जुन रामपाल ने इसमें इल्यास कश्मीरी का रोल किया है.

जानते हैं कौन था इल्यास कश्मीरी?

पाकिस्तानी सेना का अफसर था....वहां की SSG team का हिस्सा था... बाद में आतंकवादी बन गया था.

फ़रवरी 2000 में...इल्यास कश्मीरी ने LOC पार करके भारतीय post पर हमला करवाया था... जिसमे 17th मराठा Infantry के 7 जवान मारे गए थे.

और एक जवान का सर काट कर ले गया था वो....और वो ले जाकर उसने दिया था तत्कालीन पाकिस्तान आर्मी चीफ परवेज मुशररफ को.... इसके बदले उसे 1 लाख रूपए का इनाम भी मिला था.

अब ऐसे इंसान को दिखाएंगे तो क्या उसमे खून खराबा नहीं होगा?

फ़िल्म में शुरू में ही एक सीन है... जहाँ अर्जुन रामपाल ने एक भारतीय जासूस को लेटा रखा है.. उसके शरीर पर ढेरों घाव और छेद हैं.... लोग इसे भी भयावह बता रहे हैं.

क्या आप भूल गए हैं.... पाकिस्तान की फ़ौज ने कारगिल युद्ध के दौरान हमारे जवानों और अफसरों के साथ क्या किया था? उनके मृत शरीरों के साथ क्या किया था?

सच को दिखाना पड़ता है... तभी जनता को समझ आता है कि चल क्या रहा है.

मोरारजी देसाई और इंद्र कुमार गुजराल ने अपनी हरकतो से पाकिस्तान को RAW के operation के बारे में बताया दिया था.. जिसके बाद ISI ने RAW के सैंकड़ो Agents को रातो रात मार दिया था.

ये अज्ञात हमलावर तो कुछ ही सालों से पब्लिक में discuss किये जा रहे हैं... वरना पहले तो कोई बात भी नहीं करता था.

हमारे NSA अजीत डोभाल खुद पाकिस्तान में 7 साल अंडरकवर रह कर आये थे.... और भी कई अफसर, agents और गुमनाम लोगों की मिलिजुली कहानी है धुरंधर

author: @SaviVlogs18
text:
The movie Dhurandhar is a high-octane spy action thriller inspired by real-life covert operations, centering on Ranveer Singh’s character infiltrating terror networks in Pakistan. It blends fact and fiction, drawing from the story of decorated Indian Army officer Major Mohit Sharma and Pakistan’s Operation Lyari crackdown
Storyline - The film is “based on incredible true events,” weaving together elements from India-Pakistan espionage conflicts and Operation Lyari, Pakistan’s crackdown on Karachi’s gang wars between 2012–2017.  
- Ranveer Singh’s Role: He plays a covert operative modeled on Major Mohit Sharma, an Ashok Chakra awardee who infiltrated Hizbul Mujahideen under the alias Iftikhar Bhat before being martyred in Kashmir.  
  

---

⚔️ Themes & Tone
- Espionage & Patriotism: Focus on sacrifice, undercover missions, and India’s fight against terrorism.  
- Action & Drama: Stylized fight sequences, covert operations, and gritty depictions of gang wars.  
- Controversy: Pakistani audiences criticized the trailer for misrepresenting Lyari’s gang conflicts and turning them into exaggerated espionage plots.

author: @AshiqNRazal
text:
We should also understand the political context of India at the time of the Mumbai terror attacks! The time when Mumbai ATS chief Hemant Karkare discovered that the Samjotha express blast and the Malegaon blast were carried out by Hindutva forces and that an organization called Abhinav Bharat was responsible! The time when Thakur, Colonel Purohit and Swami Aseemanand were arrested as accused! The time when the country recognized saffron terrorism as well as Islamic terrorist groups! That is when the Mumbai terror attacks took place. With this, the entire attention of the country shifted away from this. Hemant Karkare, who was not on duty that night, was called in. Hemant Karkare was killed that fateful night. Although the Mumbai police said that he was killed by Pakistani terrorists from a distance, the postmortem revealed that the bullet that hit his body came from close range. His wife Kavita Karkare also raised doubts about this. But the UPA government that ruled the center at that time did not conduct a proper investigation, in other words, even at that time, Hindutva forces had enough influence in the investigation team! This is indicated in the book Who Killed Karkare by the then l G SM Mushrif.  The mastermind of this blast, David Calman Headly, is still in America. Even European countries have expressed suspicion that he is a CIA agent. Although the suicide bomber Ajmal Kasab was killed, the US did not extradite the mastermind, Headly, to India.

Instead of thrilling the people by releasing propaganda films, we need to find the black hands behind this terror and punish the mastermind, David Headley. The political parties' links with Pakistani terrorists should also be exposed.

author: @bringmethanos8728
text:
As a Telugu guy, I was always interested in Dhurandhar because it’s an Aditya Dhar film. But honestly, I wasn’t sure if I would watch it in theatres—I live in Telangana, there’s a language barrier, and I didn’t have anyone to go with.

But after the movie released, my entire timeline was flooded with memes and reviews from Telugu people—far more than even Hindi audiences. That hype alone pushed me to go.

So I finally went with my non-Hindi speaking friends…
And we were absolutely blown away.

The blood-boiling moments, the high testosterone energy, the brutal realities, the subtle references, the iconic entries, the music, the sad–funny–energetic mix of emotions, the aggressive graphics, the patriotism, the nationalism, the vision, the setup and payoffs—
Dhurandhar has everything, executed with near perfection.

Thank you Aditya Dhar — this film was truly needed 🫂
I genuinely didn’t expect it to be this special.

author: @theparanoidcritic5743
text: This is the first time Bollywood has picked a Pakistani gang war as a premise, I have real hopes for a thrilling ride. The trailer and bgm are dope, let's see if they keep the story intact. Issi bahaney Pakistan ko qareeb se dekhne samajhne ka mauka milega

author: @IlmaHussain-sh6yw
text:
@theparanoidcritic5743 respectfully, I don't have very high hopes. I don't think aapko Pakistan ko qareeb se samajne ka mauqa mile ga because at the end of the day, the facts will be twisted and Pakistan's story will be converted into an Indian story. You know for us, Karachiites, Lyari is not just a story about gang wars but it is also about the resilience the people of Karachi showed at that time because it was a very traumatising time for us and our city was never the same after that. I do not believe that the director would show the struggles we went through at that time and how complex the story of Lyari really was. At the end of the day, it will be a hyper nationalistic Indian movie with Lyari in the background only to make it appear different and interesting. 

The only thing that I like this time though that for the first time in a Bollywood movie, Pakistani villians have character and a commanding presence and they are being played by serious actors otherwise, they are always very badly written and underplayed in Bollywood movies.

author: @syedhamza9580
text:
Guess what? As a Karachiite, I actually lived through that era of Karachi. And even though the city is huge and that gang operated mainly in one of its biggest town i.e. Leyaari, their terror spread across all of Karachi even Hyderabad. In my peak teenage years, we saw so much brutality and honestly, most of what they showed in the trailer is true.

I really hope Bollywood does justice to the role of the late Chaudhry Aslam. He was a hero may God bless his soul. He was unbelievably fearless; we witnessed his bravery with our own eyes so many times. He would go after these Lyari gang criminals without backup without hesitation. He survived multiple attacks and played a huge role in taking down these criminals
